title: Exodus 10
subtitle: Plagues Eight and Nine — Locusts; Darkness

heb.paragraph: V.4-6,13-15: The eighth plague — ʾarbeh (locusts) — is described with devastating precision: they cover every visible inch of the ground, eat everything left by the hail, and fill every house. Joel 1-2 uses a locust plague as the paradigm for divine judgment. John's Revelation borrows locust imagery for the fifth trumpet (Rev 9:3-11). The agricultural annihilation of Egypt presages the spiritual desolation of those who resist the Creator.

V.21-23: The ninth plague — ḥōshek mĕʾōshek (darkness that can be felt) — inverts the creation of Gen 1:3 ("Let there be light"). Egypt is plunged into three days of total darkness while "all the Israelites had light in the places where they lived." This is not merely meteorological but ontological: the God who created light by word can remove it just as absolutely. The plague is a creation-reversal announcing the coming uncreation of Pharaoh's firstborn.

panels.hist: Locust swarms (Schistocerca gregaria) are documented in Egyptian administrative texts — one papyrus describes crops consumed, starvation following. The eastern wind (v.13: "the LORD brought an east wind upon the land") is the sirocco (khamsin) — a hot wind from the Arabian desert that periodically devastates Egyptian agriculture. The western wind that removed the locusts (v.19) is equally natural — and equally under YHWH's command.
panels.ctx: Two new elements appear in plague eight: (1) the explicit memorial purpose — "that you may tell your son and grandson" (v.2) — establishes the Exodus as a story to be retold in every generation; (2) Pharaoh's officials break ranks and urge capitulation (v.7: "Egypt is ruined"). Pharaoh's court has reached the same conclusion as the magicians in plague three — but Pharaoh still hardens his heart. He is now isolated even from his own advisors.

cross.note: The locust plague becomes Joel's paradigm for eschatological judgment — "a nation has invaded my land, powerful and without number."
The fifth trumpet releases locusts as eschatological judgment — the Exodus plague as end-time template.
The "tell your children" command of 10:2 is fulfilled in the Passover ritual: "What does this ceremony mean to you?" — the institutionalised retelling of the Exodus.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The intergenerational purpose announced in v.2 is foundational to Israelite worship. Sarna identifies this as the first explicit commandment to tell children about the Exodus — a command that becomes the structural principle of the Passover Seder (Exod 13:8–9; Deut 6:20–25). The plagues are designed not only as historical events but as memorable, retellable demonstrations that will shape Israelite identity across all future generations.

The east wind that brings the locusts is the qadim — the hot, dry desert wind from the Arabian Peninsula. Sarna notes that while locust plagues were historically known in Egypt, they were extremely rare; the region's climate typically produced western winds. An east wind strong enough to carry a locust swarm of this density from the east is climatologically extraordinary — another sign that natural forces are under divine direction.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The servants' intervention — 'Do you not yet understand that Egypt is ruined?' — is a dramatic shift in the narrative. For the first time, voices within Pharaoh's own court plead for capitulation. Alter notes the political realism: the servants are not theologians; they are pragmatists who have witnessed enough to calculate the outcome. Their plea makes Pharaoh's subsequent hardening even more wilful — he is now defying not only God but his own advisors.

Pharaoh's final compromise — take everyone but leave the livestock — is met by Moses' most absolute refusal: 'not a hoof shall be left behind.' The negotiating positions have reached their limits. Both parties know it. Pharaoh's expulsion of Moses in v.28 is the breaking point; the next chapter will be the final act.

Calvin on the intergenerational purpose: 'God wished his mighty power to be celebrated throughout all ages, lest the memory of so great a benefit should perish.' The plagues are designed for retelling — they create the content of Israelite worship, the stories that will be rehearsed at Passover tables for millennia. The didactic purpose of the Exodus is built into the plague narrative from the beginning.

Pharaoh's confession after the locusts — 'I have sinned against the LORD your God and against you' — is his most elaborate verbal capitulation. Calvin notes the specificity: 'against the LORD your God and against you' names both dimensions of the sin. But the pattern holds: confession under pressure, betrayal under relief. Calvin reads the elaborate confession as evidence of how deeply the conscience can be moved without the will being changed.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on locusts (arbeh) explains the term derives from the root rabah ('to be many') — locusts are named for their multitude. The note discusses the historical ecology: locust swarms of biblical proportions (covering the land so it was 'darkened') are documented in North Africa and the Middle East. A swarm can cover hundreds of square miles and consume vegetation completely. The plague's scale, timing, and direction (east wind, then west wind removing them) are the signs of divine direction.

The NET note on the darkness plague notes that complete darkness — 'darkness that could be felt' — for three days is not naturally occurring in Egypt. The note discusses the khamsin (a dusty windstorm that can reduce visibility) as a possible natural mechanism intensified divinely, but notes that three days of total darkness affecting Egypt while Goshen had light is not reducible to any natural phenomenon.

MacArthur: Go to Pharaoh... so that you may tell your children and grandchildren how I dealt with the Egyptians. The plagues are designed to generate generational testimony. The story is to be told and retold; the Passover Seder institutionalises this retelling.

MacArthur: Do you not yet realize that Egypt is ruined? -- Pharaoh's officials have arrived at the conclusion that Pharaoh refuses. The isolation of Pharaoh's stubbornness highlights the personal nature of hardening.

sections.header: Verses 21—29 — Plague Nine: Darkness; Moses Warns of the Final Plague

heb.paragraph: V.21: "Darkness that can be felt" (ḥōshek ʾĕphelyāh — lit. "darkness of darkness"). The intensification by doubling is emphatic: this is total, palpable darkness, not dim light. In Isa 60:2 "darkness covers the earth" before the light of the LORD rises on Zion. The plague simultaneously attacks Ra, the Egyptian sun-god, and foreshadows the Gospel's contrast: light has come into the world (John 1:5) and the darkness has not overcome it.

V.11: Pharaoh's expulsion of Moses after the locust plague — "Get out of my sight!" — uses the verb gārash (to drive out, expel), the same word used when God drove out Adam from Eden (Gen 3:24). The irony is complete: Pharaoh thinks he is expelling the threat; he is actually positioning himself for the final reversal where he will beg Israel to leave.

panels.hist: The plague of darkness lasting three days attacks Ra, the supreme deity of the Egyptian pantheon. Egyptian theology held that Ra's daily journey across the sky sustained all life. Three days of impenetrable darkness — 'darkness that could be felt' — represented cosmic chaos, the return of the primordial darkness before creation. The Egyptians called this state isfet (disorder, chaos), the opposite of maat (order, truth). Pharaoh's response (offering to let the people go but keep the livestock) reflects the negotiation pattern common in ANE diplomatic exchanges.
panels.ctx: The darkness plague is no-warning (like the boils) and reaches the deepest theological point of the plague sequence: Ra, the sun-god, the supreme deity of Egypt, whose image Pharaoh bears as divine son — YHWH extinguishes him for three days. The darkness is "to be felt" — not merely visual absence but a presence of darkness. Three days echoes creation (light on day one) and anticipates the three days of Jonah and the three days of Christ in the tomb. The final verse — Pharaoh's threat and Moses's acceptance of it — sets up the last and greatest plague.

cross.note: "Let there be light" — YHWH is the creator of light. The darkness plague reverses creation in Egypt while preserving it in Goshen. YHWH does with Egypt's sun what he did with the world's light: he controls it absolutely.
The pillar of cloud: light to Israel, darkness to Egypt. The same polarity at the Red Sea.
Three hours of darkness at the crucifixion — the ninth plague's three days of darkness as typological background.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The darkness plague is directed against Ra, the sun god, Egypt's most important deity. Sarna notes that the Pharaoh himself was considered the son of Ra — the supreme identification between divine and royal in Egyptian religion. Darkness for three days was not merely an inconvenience; it was the annihilation of the divine power that defined Egyptian civilisation. The light in Goshen is the counter-sign: YHWH's people live in light while the sun-god's people sit in darkness.

Pharaoh's final offer — 'go, worship the LORD, only leave your flocks and herds' — and Moses' refusal constitute the last negotiation before the final plague. Sarna notes Moses' answer is theologically precise: 'We do not know what we will use to worship the LORD until we arrive there.' Worship requires full freedom; no pre-commitment to what God will require.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The darkness plague is narrated with unusual physicality: 'darkness that could be felt' (v.21), 'a thick darkness' (v.22), no one rose from his place for three days. Alter notes the immobility — people literally unable to move — is the plague's most powerful expression. The society that built monuments with forced labour is now paralysed. The contrast with Israel ('had light in their dwellings') is the sharpest discrimination in the narrative.

The final negotiation breaks down irreparably: Pharaoh's offer leaves the livestock; Moses refuses; Pharaoh says 'if you appear before me again you will die.' Alter notes the dramatic irony: this is the last time Moses will see Pharaoh. The death threat seals the relationship's end before the final plague.

Calvin reads the palpable darkness as a judgment on Egyptian unbelief: 'Let us learn that nothing is more miserable than to be left by God in the darkness of our own mind.' The physical darkness mirrors the spiritual darkness that Pharaoh has inhabited throughout the plague narrative. Egypt's sun-god cannot produce light; the God of Israel provides it specifically for his people. The physical and spiritual dimensions of the sign correspond precisely.

Calvin on the three days of darkness: 'The light of the sun was extinguished for three days — not that the sun ceased its course, but God intercepted the sun's light from reaching Egypt. This miracle was specifically directed against the Egyptian sun worship.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'darkness that can be felt' (choshek afelah) explains afelah is an intensified form of darkness — absolute, impenetrable blackness. The note discusses whether this could refer to a severe khamsin (dust storm) lasting three days, which would explain the physicality. But the discrimination (light in Goshen) exceeds any natural explanation. The note notes that three-day complete darkness is without known natural parallel for the Nile Delta region.

The NET note on 'not a hoof is to be left behind' discusses the legal and theological point: worship requires bringing everything that might be needed. Moses cannot pre-commit what sacrifices the LORD will require before arriving at the place of worship. The demand for complete freedom is not stubbornness but covenant principle.

MacArthur: Three days of palpable darkness over Egypt while the Israelites had light in their dwellings is direct judgment on Ra, the sun god, Egypt's chief deity. The plague sequence's most concentrated contrast of judgment and grace.

MacArthur: The three days of palpable darkness is a direct assault on Ra, Egypt's supreme deity. The god who was supposed to guarantee the sun's daily journey across the sky is rendered impotent. Light in Goshen during the darkness makes the theological point unmissable: YHWH governs the light Ra claimed to produce.

ppl.name: Pharaoh
ppl.text: (2) Pharaoh's officials break ranks and urge capitulation (v…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationESV"Stretch out your hand toward heaven, that there may be darkness over the land of Egypt, a darkness to be felt... There was pitch darkness in all the land of Egypt three days... but all the people of Israel had light where they lived."NIV"Stretch out your hand toward the sky so that darkness spreads over Egypt—darkness that can be felt... Yet all the Israelites had light in the places where they lived."

src.title: Ra and Pharaonic Theology
src.quote: Pharaoh was considered the divine son of Ra (the sun-god), the embodiment of his rule on earth. Ra's daily journey across the sky was the theological cornerstone of Egyptian religion.
src.note: The darkness plague targets Egyptian theology at its pinnacle — the supreme deity's primary domain.

src.title: Khamsin / Sirocco
src.quote: The hot desert wind (khamsin) can bring sandstorms that blot out the sun for days — known as "the darkness that can be felt." Whether the plague was a hypernatural khamsin or something entirely different, the description matches an Egyptian meteorological phenomenon taken to an extreme.
src.note: The "felt darkness" description is culturally grounded.

rec.text: Three hours of darkness at the crucifixion during Jesus's death — early Christian interpreters read this as the darkness plague's typological fulfilment: as YHWH darkened Egypt's sun-god in judgment, so the sun is darkened at the death of the Son of God.
The fifth bowl judgment darkens the throne of the beast — the darkness plague as eschatological template, targeting the center of anti-God power.

rows.label: Darkness: Three Days of Palpable Night
rows.text: Darkness that could be felt -- three days of complete darkness over Egypt while Israel had light in their dwellings. The plague is simultaneously a judgment on Ra and a preview of the Passover's final night.

rows.label: The Final Negotiation: All or Nothing
rows.text: Pharaoh offers: go, but leave your livestock. Moses refuses: not a hoof will be left behind. We cannot know what we will need to worship. The final negotiation breaks down into a death threat -- and the last plague becomes inevitable.

The fifth trumpet releases locusts as eschatological judgment — the Exodus plague as end-time template.

tx.title: Hardening of Pharaoh's heart — SP expansions
tx.content: The SP adds substantial content at the beginning of each plague cycle, harmonizing God's instructions to Moses with the narrative that follows. In Exodus 10, the SP inserts dialogue drawn from later in the narrative, ensuring that God's command matches its execution word for word.
tx.note: The SP's plague narrative expansions are among its most extensive divergences from MT. They reveal a scribal commitment to narrative consistency that MT does not share.

debate.title: Pharaoh's Servants' Role in the Hardening Narrative

positions.name: Servants as morally culpable accomplices
positions.argument: Exodus 9:34 explicitly states 'he and his servants hardened their hearts.' The servants are morally responsible participants in the corporate resistance to God. Their later plea (10:7) does not exonerate them — it merely shows their pragmatic calculation has changed while Pharaoh's has not.

positions.name: Servants as narrative device for escalation
positions.argument: The servants' plea in 10:7 functions narratively to isolate Pharaoh — he is now resisting not only God and Moses but his own court. The servants are less developed characters than Pharaoh; their shift marks the political isolation that precedes Pharaoh's total defeat in ch.14.
